EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure across the region brought extreme heat and humidity to the Mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport, KDCA, observed a peak head index of 110F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked south of the region across the Carolinas. This brought a period of light to moderate snow to the District of Columbia during the late evening hours of January 10th through the predawn of January 11th. Snowfall accumulations ranged 1 to 2 inches.

As an area of low pressure moved north across the Ohio Valley, cold air remained to the east of the Alleghenies. This resulted in a period of freezing rain for the District of Columbia. Ice accumulations were a coating to a few hundredths of an inch across the District.

As an area of low pressure moved north across the Ohio Valley, cold air remained to the east of the Alleghenies. A mix of snow and sleet initially occurred with a general coating across the District of Columbia. This transitioned to a mix of freezing rain and sleet which continued through the evening resulting in a glaze of ice.
Behind a potent cold front, strong gradient winds developed across much of the region during the late morning to afternoon hours on February 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Reagan National Airport, KDCA, measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph at 1624 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air moved in behind a departing low pressure system on January 20. This Arctic air remained overhead as high pressure dominated through January 22. Winds gusted to 25-35 mph at times bringing apparent temperatures/wind chill values as low as -10F across the District of Columbia. Air temperatures fell into the single digits at night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coldest apparent temperatures/wind chills overnight into morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across southern Virginia bringing the first widespread accumulating snow of the season. Snow overspread the area during the late evening hours of January 5th into the overnight. Snow overspread the area during the late evening hours of January 5th into the overnight. This continued steady through mid-morning. A lull occurred during the afternoon with a few snow showers. Another round of snow on the back side of the upper level low moved in during the evening bringing additional accumulations. This resulted in of 7 to 10 inches of snow across the District of Columbia. Winds gusted to 45 mph resulting in blowing snow at the end of the storm.
An area of low pressure moved off into New England bringing a cold front through Virginia. Snow showers and squalls along the front brought snow accumulations of a coating to an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall with rapid drop is visibility below one quarter of a mile, winds greater than 25 mph, and quick accumulation of snow on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rains in the headwaters of the Potomac River in western Maryland in mid May, particularly at Westernport MD lead to excessive runoff on the Potomac River that led to river flooding downstream on the Potomac River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rains in the headwaters of the Potomac River in western Maryland in mid May, particularly at Westernport MD, led to excessive runoff that resulted in river flooding downstream eventually reaching Washington DC on the 15th. The tidal flooding continued for multiple tide cycles reaching a crest of 8.8' late on the 15th at 1118 PM EST. Lead time for this event was 16.6 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms in northwest flow produced several instances of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least a half dozen trees blew down in Northwest DC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms in northwest flow produced several instances of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees blew down near Columbia Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather outbreak occurred during the mid to late afternoon hours of Thursday, June 19th, 2025, as a strong cold front supported by a seasonably strong upper-level jet intersected a hot, humid, and unstable airmass. Severe weather was exclusively in the form of damaging wind gusts, many of which were considerable (near hurricane-force) in nature in the Washington DC metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees blew down on 16th Street Northwest at Alaska Avenue Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure across the region brought extreme heat and humidity to the Mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport, KDCA, observed a peak head index of 110F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere along with plenty of moisture led to the development of showers and thunderstorms. The thunderstorms were slow moving, and trained over the same areas in spots resulting in flash flooding. Rainfall amounts of one to locally four inches were observed in Northwest. Rainfall from the Sligo Creek watershed resulted in a rapid rise along the Anacostia River, while rainfall on the Rock Creek watershed resulted in a rapid rise along Rock Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge upstream along Rock Creek rose about 6 feet within an hour. The water was estimated to have flowed through the rest of Rock Creek into the Potomac River, causing rapid rises along the way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere along with plenty of moisture led to the development of showers and thunderstorms. The thunderstorms were slow moving, and trained over the same areas in spots resulting in flash flooding. Rainfall amounts of one to locally four inches were observed in Northwest. Rainfall from the Sligo Creek watershed resulted in a rapid rise along the Anacostia River, while rainfall on the Rock Creek watershed resulted in a rapid rise along Rock Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge upstream along Sligo Creek rose about 6 feet within an hour. Montgomery County Emergency Management indicated about 10 foot rises in the same area with their gauges. This water drains into the Anacostia River. The water was estimated to have flowed through the rest of the Anacostia into the Potomac River, causing rapid rises along the way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously moist airmass ahead of a frontal boundary sparked numerous showers and thunderstorms across the District of Columbia. Instantaneous rain rates approached five to six inches an hour during the evening, but were transient. Storms still dropped a quick one to two inches in twenty minutes, resulting in flash flooding throughout the District.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least six inches of water flowing across Sherman Avenue NW in front of DCFD Engine Company 4 firehouse.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicle stranded in bottom of Calvert Street NW at bottom of hill. 1.5 feet of water with stalled vehicle, no rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed early in the afternoon in an anomalously moist airmass along the Chesapeake Bay Breeze and remnant outflow boundaries. These storms remained nearly stationary through the first part of the afternoon producing a quick two to four inches of rain before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Hampshire Avenue NW closed between 4th and 5th |            street NW and Webster St NW due to at least 6 to 12 |            inches of water flowing across the roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 7.42 foot rise at NAHD2 (Hickey Run at National |            Arboretum) in 50 minutes (1238 PM through 128 PM).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front intersected a hot and very humid airmass resulting in several clusters of severe thunderstorms across the Mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down onto an unoccupied vehicle at the intersection of Ellicott Street Northwest and 32nd Street Northwest. A large tree blew down near the intersection of Connecticut Avenue Northwest and Chesapeake Street Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalling cold front resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms. Localized swaths of damaging wind were observed over northern Virginia and Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were around 30 calls for trees blown down across the District of Columbia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front intersected a hot, humid, and unstable airmass over the Mid-Atlantic resulting in widely scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blew down in the 800 block of Atlantic Street Southeast, and in the 400 block of Brandywine Street Southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moved across the Mid-Atlantic bringing a swath of one to three inches of snow across the District of Columbia. Temperatures were in the 20s going into the event resulting in snow sticking to most untreated surfaces. This impacted the morning commute and resulted in numerous accidents.

A light wintry mix of snow and freezing drizzle developed during the evening of December 5th. This continued through the overnight before tapering off by daybreak December 6th. Snowfall amounts were generally a coating to an inch with a glaze to few hundredths of an inch or ice.
